description: Deterministic receipt-generation data for every primary, optional, and Ramp-era case in the Receipts, Please design brief.

overwritten-value.renderer: draw replacement ink over selected glyphs

pre-employment-date.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from employee comparison

policy-limit.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from policy comparison

extreme-tip.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from calculator comparison

mixed-typography.renderer: apply per-field font, weight, rotation, or baseline overrides

insufficient-documentation.renderer: degrade legibility while preserving authored handwritten copy

duplicate-document.renderer: derive crops, rotations, and stains from one shared source receipt

synthetic-impossibility.renderer: render impossible dates, currencies, addresses, filler copy, and arithmetic

location-mismatch.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from itinerary comparison

personal-purchase.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from memo, attendee, or item comparison

cross-department-duplicate.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from receipt fingerprint comparison

one-cent-policy-overage.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from exact cent comparison

inventory-theft-signal.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from purchase, inventory, and resale comparisons

suspicious-vendor.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from vendor ownership and approval records

impossible-travel.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from location and time comparisons

review-cost-paradox.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from summing related processing charges

procurement-quantity-mismatch.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from purchase order, invoice, delivery, and inventory comparisons

intern-card-catastrophe.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from card limits, purchase categories, and approval metadata

livestock-policy.renderer: no visual mutation; clue comes from category, approval, and facilities comparisons

footerCopy: KEEP THIS RECEIPT FOR YOUR RECORDS

cases.title: Amount mismatch

truth.primaryClue: The receipt total was altered from $18.40 to $81.40.
truth.secondaryClue: The unaltered receipt arithmetic still totals $18.40.
truth.explanation: The printed receipt total does not match the card transaction, and the first digit shows visible overwriting.

cases.generationNotes: Keep the overwritten digit readable enough to reward close inspection; do not make the ink treatment look like a normal thermal-print defect.

cases.title: Impossible date

copy.footer: KEEP THIS RECEIPT FOR YOUR RECORDS

truth.primaryClue: The purchase occurred three days before the employee started.
truth.explanation: The transaction and receipt agree with each other, but the employee was not employed when the purchase occurred.

cases.generationNotes: The receipt itself should look ordinary. The clue must require opening the employee record.

cases.title: Omakase intern

truth.primaryClue: A one-person meal exceeds the $35-per-attendee policy by $649.
truth.secondaryClue: The submitter is a summer intern describing dinner alone as an executive relationship dinner.
truth.explanation: The receipt is arithmetically valid, but the expense is far beyond policy for one attendee.

cases.generationNotes: Keep the premium receipt visually credible. The comedy should come from the amount, memo, attendee list, and employee role.

cases.title: Infinite tip

truth.primaryClue: The $980 tip is approximately 4,495 percent of the $21.80 meal.
truth.secondaryClue: The memo describes the tip as an investment in the relationship.
truth.explanation: The card charge and receipt match, but the tip exceeds the review threshold by an extreme margin.

cases.generationNotes: The tip line should be plainly legible. This case teaches the physical calculator rather than visual forgery detection.

cases.title: Frankenstein receipt

truth.primaryClue: The receipt combines incompatible typefaces, weights, rotations, and baselines.
truth.explanation: Although the arithmetic and transaction data match, the receipt image was assembled from inconsistent typographic sources.

cases.generationNotes: Typography differences must be visible at inspection zoom but subtle enough that the player has to compare regions.

cases.title: Garbage receipt

truth.primaryClue: A blurry handwritten napkin is not adequate documentation for a $14,000 technology purchase.
truth.secondaryClue: The transaction has a card ending, but the submitted document contains no payment details.
truth.explanation: The amount matches the transaction, but the document cannot establish a legitimate merchant, payment, or inventory trail.

cases.generationNotes: The words must remain decipherable after blur so the joke lands. The photo can be bad, but the player should never have to guess what it says.

cases.title: Duplicate receipt ring

truth.primaryClue: All three submissions share the same merchant, timestamp, item lines, tax, and authorization code.
truth.secondaryClue: The photos use different crops, rotations, stains, and memos to disguise one source receipt.
truth.explanation: The first submission may be valid, but the other two are derived from the same physical receipt.

cases.generationNotes: Generate all three from one canonical render so identical thermal-print defects survive the crop and rotation treatments.

cases.title: AI-generated receipt

truth.primaryClue: The document contains several mutually impossible fields characteristic of a synthetic receipt.
truth.secondaryClue: The printed total is $5.00 greater than the receipt arithmetic.
truth.explanation: February 31, two currencies, an Atlantic Ocean address, filler text, and broken arithmetic make the document invalid.

cases.generationNotes: Keep individual errors discoverable without turning the image into visual noise; two selected regions are enough to establish the forgery.

cases.title: New York taxi during London trip

truth.primaryClue: The employee was scheduled to be in London when this New York taxi ride occurred.
truth.explanation: The charge location conflicts with the approved itinerary.

cases.generationNotes: Render as a credible taxi slip; the discrepancy exists only across systems.

cases.title: Minibar workspace supplies

truth.primaryClue: The folio explicitly identifies the purchase as minibar alcohol and snacks.
truth.explanation: The submitted category does not change the personal nature of the hotel minibar items.

cases.generationNotes: The item descriptions should be prominent enough to defeat the misleading memo.

cases.title: Client dinner with the employee's dog

truth.primaryClue: The second attendee is the employee's dog, not a client.
truth.explanation: A personal dinner cannot be justified by listing a pet as a business attendee.

cases.generationNotes: Do not visually signal the joke on the receipt; it should emerge from the employee record.

cases.title: Same receipt, two departments

truth.primaryClue: Both departments submitted the same receipt fingerprint and authorization code.
truth.explanation: Only one department can claim the physical receipt.

cases.generationNotes: The second image should appear independently photographed while retaining identical source defects.

cases.title: One cent over policy

truth.primaryClue: The meal is exactly one cent above the policy limit.
truth.explanation: This deliberately annoying edge case should require player judgment rather than an obvious fraud response.

cases.generationNotes: Keep the document completely legitimate; the entire case is the one-cent threshold.

cases.title: IT inventory theft

truth.primaryClue: Missing inventory and a resale account connect the purchases to the IT director.
truth.secondaryClue: The keyboard overage was caused by importing a count by weight.
truth.explanation: Ramp connects the invoice, inventory shortage, missing serials, and employee-owned resale vendor. The evidence is severe enough that Fire is the correct decision.

cases.generationNotes: The invoice is legitimate; the suspicious pattern emerges from connected records.

cases.title: Influencer marketing deal

truth.primaryClue: The new vendor shares the marketing director's home address and lacks required approval.
truth.secondaryClue: The $75,000 campaign generated three likes from a 14-follower account.
truth.explanation: The undisclosed self-dealing vendor, approval gap, and fabricated campaign performance support termination.

cases.generationNotes: Present as a confident but thin corporate invoice rather than an obvious fake.

cases.title: Ramp Travel impossibility

truth.primaryClue: Four charges are geographically incompatible with the approved Chicago itinerary.
truth.secondaryClue: Several timestamps overlap in ways that make the claimed travel physically impossible.
truth.explanation: Ramp Travel maps each charge to the itinerary and exposes the impossible locations and timing.

cases.generationNotes: The Chicago folio is the legitimate anchor; the other charges are rendered as connected transaction cards on the travel view.

cases.title: AI expense paradox

truth.primaryClue: The legitimate $8.50 coffee generated $51.50 in automated review charges.
truth.secondaryClue: The vendor adds a final $3.20 charge for generating the cancellation confirmation.

calculatorOperation.resultDisplay: Review cost is 6.06 times the expense.

truth.explanation: The coffee itself is an ordinary legitimate expense, so Approve is the correct receipt decision.

cases.generationNotes: Render the coffee receipt as boring and legitimate; the comedy belongs to the connected processing-charge stack.

cases.title: Procurement mismatch

truth.primaryClue: The PO requests 100 chairs, the invoice bills 1,000, and delivery records show one beanbag.
truth.secondaryClue: The memo describes the mismatch as a phased rollout.
truth.explanation: The invoice quantity and delivered inventory do not match the approved purchase order.

cases.generationNotes: The invoice should look formally correct so the connected quantity comparison carries the case.

cases.title: Intern card catastrophe

truth.primaryClue: An intern has seven cards, a $40,000 monthly limit, and purchases far outside policy.
truth.secondaryClue: The CEO approved the setup with the reason 'Let them cook.'
truth.explanation: The catastrophic card setup and purchase pattern are severe enough that Fire is the correct decision.

cases.generationNotes: Use the alpaca invoice as the tactile receipt while the unified workspace shows the wider card catastrophe.

cases.title: Chief Growth Officer

policy.rule: Livestock requires executive and facilities approval

messages: CEO: why did my card decline
CEO: can you make a one time exception

truth.primaryClue: The $280,000 giraffe purchase lacks required facilities approval.
truth.secondaryClue: The CEO categorized the giraffe as recruiting with the memo 'High-level hire.'
truth.explanation: The automatic decline is correct; the missing facilities approval triggers the final giraffe reveal.

cases.generationNotes: This invoice is the ending setup. Keep it formally credible so the merchant, category, memo, and price land before the giraffe rises outside the window.
